fact_text: Broadway follows an older Native path through Manhattan and long predates the Commissioners' grid.
Wall Street takes its name from the defensive wall the Dutch built across northern New Amsterdam in 1653.
The Bowery grew out of a Dutch farm road and remains one of Manhattan's oldest thoroughfares.
Fifth Avenue became the line that divided Manhattan's east and west street addresses and later one of the city's signature boulevards.
Houston Street marks the rough divide between much of Uptown and Downtown Manhattan and is named for statesman William Houstoun.
Canal Street was built over the filled-in path of the polluted Collect Pond outflow canal.
Delancey Street is named for James De Lancey Sr., whose estate once covered much of what became the Lower East Side.
Chrystie Street was renamed for Colonel John Chrystie and later gave its name to the 1967 subway connection linking former BMT and IND lines.
Bleecker Street was named for the prominent Bleecker family, whose farm once covered part of this area.
Stuyvesant Street is one of the few Manhattan streets that still preserves a pre-grid alignment from the Dutch and early republic city.
Lafayette Street honors the Marquis de Lafayette and was opened as part of a 19th-century north-south traffic improvement.
Fulton Street was named for Robert Fulton, whose steamboat work reshaped transportation in New York.
Orchard Street took its name from the old De Lancey estate orchard and later became one of the Lower East Side's signature immigrant tenement corridors.
Essex Street was laid out by James Delancey before the Revolution and later became one of the Lower East Side's best-known Jewish commercial strips.
Allen Street was widened in the 1930s, creating the broad median-lined corridor that still cuts through the Lower East Side today.
Rivington Street is named for Loyalist printer James Rivington, who secretly ran intelligence to George Washington during the Revolution.
Ludlow Street was built on land from the confiscated De Lancey estate and later became a defining Lower East Side music and nightlife strip.
Bedford Avenue is Brooklyn's longest street and grew from an older road linking Flatbush toward Newtown Creek before later urban expansion stitched it together.
Metropolitan Avenue began as the Williamsburgh and Jamaica Turnpike, an 1816 toll road built over an older Native route.
Grand Street was early Williamsburg's main east-west commercial street and helped organize the neighborhood's north and south numbering system.
Manhattan Avenue became Greenpoint's main shopping spine and is still the center of the neighborhood's historic Little Poland corridor.
Nassau Avenue became one of Greenpoint's central east-west corridors as the neighborhood shifted from farmland into a surveyed industrial district in the 19th century.
Neziah Bliss opened a public turnpike along what is now Franklin Street in 1839, helping trigger Greenpoint's rapid urban growth.
Leonard Street is part of the inland Greenpoint grid that filled in after Neziah Bliss surveyed and urbanized the neighborhood in the 1830s.
Eckford Street ran through Greenpoint's industrial buildout; Union Porcelain Works, once one of America's best-known porcelain manufacturers, stood at 300 Eckford Street.
McGuinness Boulevard was formerly Oakland Street, then widened after the Pulaski Bridge opened and renamed in 1964 for local alderman Peter McGuinness.
Newel Street is one of the north-south streets laid through Greenpoint as the old farming peninsula was divided into urban blocks during the 19th century.
Diamond Street is part of the north-south Greenpoint street grid that spread eastward as the neighborhood industrialized in the 1800s.
Russell Street now marks one edge of McGolrick Park, reflecting how Greenpoint's street grid and parkland were fixed during the neighborhood's late 19th-century growth.
Monitor Street runs near the Bushwick Creek shipbuilding zone where the ironclad USS Monitor was built, a reminder of Greenpoint's Civil War-era industrial waterfront.
Kingsland Avenue is part of eastern Greenpoint's industrial-era grid near Newtown Creek, where factories, foundries, and maritime yards shaped the neighborhood.
Sutton Street is one of the residential cross streets added as Greenpoint's eastern blocks filled in during the neighborhood's 19th-century expansion.
Morgan Avenue sits on Greenpoint's eastern edge near the old industrial belt tied to Newtown Creek and later manufacturing growth.
Hausman Street marks the far eastern side of the Nassau Avenue stretch in Greenpoint, close to the neighborhood's old industrial lands near Newtown Creek.
Wythe Avenue ran through Williamsburg's industrial waterfront district and today still traces the neighborhood's shift from factories to mixed-use redevelopment.
Kent Avenue hugs the East River edge of Williamsburg, following the corridor where sugar refining, shipping, and heavy industry once dominated the waterfront.
Berry Street is part of the 19th-century Williamsburg street grid that expanded northward as ferry-linked development accelerated along the waterfront.
Roebling Street reflects Williamsburg's industrial-era street grid and recalls the bridge-building name associated with Brooklyn's great suspension bridges.
Driggs Avenue is one of the diagonal-to-grid transition corridors in Williamsburg, running through blocks shaped by the neighborhood's 19th-century expansion.
Union Avenue became a major spine in Williamsburg and later formed part of the route toward the old Broadway ferry and inland industrial blocks.
Lorimer Street runs through the heart of Williamsburg's residential grid, an area built out rapidly as Brooklyn urbanized in the late 19th century.
Graham Avenue became one of north Brooklyn's most important immigrant commercial corridors, especially for Williamsburg's Italian community.
North 6 Street is part of the numbered street system laid out during Williamsburg's 19th-century north-side expansion.
North 7 Street belongs to Williamsburg's numbered street grid, created as the village expanded beyond its original waterfront core.
Greenpoint Avenue became one of the neighborhood's defining east-west corridors, linking the East River waterfront to Newtown Creek industry inland.
Union Square developed at the junction of Bloomingdale Road and the Bowery Road and later became one of New York's best-known civic gathering places.
Union Square Park opened in 1839 and became a long-running civic gathering ground, from the first Labor Day parade in 1882 to later protests and rallies.
Washington Square Park began as a potter's field and parade ground before becoming one of Greenwich Village's defining public spaces.
City Hall Park is one of Manhattan's oldest civic spaces and has served as a public commons since the colonial era.
Madison Square Park occupies part of the old Madison Cottage and parade-ground district that gave the square its name.
Bryant Park stands on the former site of the Croton Distributing Reservoir, once a major piece of New York's 19th-century water system.
Times Square took its modern name in 1904 when The New York Times moved its headquarters to the new Times Building.
Columbus Circle was laid out in 1905 as a major traffic circle and monument site at the southwest corner of Central Park.
When it opened in 1883, the Brooklyn Bridge was the world's longest suspension bridge and a defining feat of New York engineering.
South Street Seaport preserves part of the old East River waterfront where New York grew into a major 19th-century port city.
McCarren Park was assembled between 1903 and 1905 on land near an old Bushwick Inlet tributary and later became a shared park for Williamsburg and Greenpoint.
Domino Park opened in 2018 on the former Domino Sugar Refinery site and keeps salvaged machinery visible as a reminder of Williamsburg's industrial waterfront.
Completed in 1903, the Williamsburg Bridge briefly held the world's longest suspension span and permanently tied the Lower East Side to Brooklyn's growth.
Transmitter Park sits on a former WNYC radio transmission site on Greenpoint's waterfront, keeping the neighborhood's industrial and broadcasting history visible.
Bushwick Inlet Park occupies part of a waterfront long defined by shipbuilding, fuel depots, and heavy industry at the north edge of Williamsburg.
McGolrick Park opened in 1891 as Winthrop Park and became one of Greenpoint's major landscaped squares during the neighborhood's late 19th-century growth.
The Domino Sugar Refinery became one of Williamsburg's defining industrial landmarks and for decades was among the largest sugar refineries in the world.
